text: Riemer, R-I-E-M-E-R. The painting on the left is called in the mountains, 1928. The painting on the right is called 1928, composition with a figure. speaker: interviewer
text: Issachar Ryback, R-Y-B-A-C-K, also known as Louis Ribak, R-I-B-A-K, the director of the Taos Valley art school in New Mexico. Born in the colony Constantinava, Grodno, Gubernia, Russian Poland on December 3 1903. He emigrated to the USA in 1912, married to the painter Beatrice Mandelman. The painting shown is [? painted in Russia in 1925.

text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] on the left. On the right from 1930. [PAUSES FOR 18 SECONDS] 1930 letter from Paris. [PAUSES FOR 27 SECONDS] 1931 letter from Paris. Top right is a self portrait of the artist, 1929. Bottom left, landscape. Center, Bottom right, portrait of the artist's grandmother. [PAUSES FOR 24 SECONDS] 1930 letter from Krakow. [PAUSES FOR 30 SECONDS] 1930 letter from Krakow. [PAUSES FOR 27 SECONDS] Pages two and three. [PAUSES FOR 29 SECONDS] Page four. [PAUSES FOR 18 SECONDS] Letter from Paris, 1934. [PAUSES FOR 32 SECONDS] Page two. [PAUSES FOR 28 SECONDS] Alis Guggenheim, G-U-G-G-E-N-H-E-I-M. born in 1896. Top left, terracotta bust, 1928. Middle top, 1924. Top right, terracotta boy from 1930. Bottom left, terracotta, 1928. Bottom center, a painting of flowers. Bottom right, from 1931. [PAUSES FOR 15 SECONDS] Letter from Alis Guggenheim to Otto Schneid, April 1934. [PAUSES FOR 23 SECONDS] Page two. [PAUSES FOR 30 SECONDS] Page three. [PAUSES FOR 30 SECONDS] Page four. [PAUSES FOR 25 SECONDS] Rivka Avivi-Stark, A-V-I-V-I-S-T-A-R-K, born 1895 in Russia. She went to Palestine when she was 19 years old. Shown are examples of her craft. She was a doll maker, and the bottom center picture is a photograph of the artist with some of her dolls. [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] Iosif Iser, I-S-E-R, born in Bucharest, 1881. Lived and worked in Paris and Bucharest during the First and Second World Wars. Shown is 1924, portrait of a Turkish women. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] On the left, On the right, [PAUSES FOR 17 SECONDS] Catalog from an exhibition in New York, 1948. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Samuel Obodowski-Ovadjahn, O-B-O-D-O-W-S-K-I-O-V-A-D-J-A-H-N, also known as Ovadia, was born in the Ukraine in 1892 and went to Palestine in the 1940s. On the left is a self portrait. On the top right is a landscape of Jerusalem. On the bottom right is Jaffa. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] Barac Szwarc, S-Z-W-A-R-C, born in Poland 1892. Went to Paris in 1910. Went back to Poland during World War I, and in 1920 returned to Paris. This is a bas-relief entitled the death, 1928. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] On the left is a photograph of the artist with his work On the right is a bust of a woman. Both pieces are from 1930. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] Letter from Paris, 1931. [PAUSES FOR 29 SECONDS] Arie Reznik, R-E-Z-N-I-K, born in Sosnowiec in 1898. He went to Palestine in 1920. Shown are six sculptures from the 1920s. [PAUSES FOR 17 SECONDS] Postcard from Vienna, 1931. [PAUSES FOR 30 SECONDS] Letter from [? Rifa ?], 1957. [PAUSES FOR 27 SECONDS] The sculptor Oscar Miestchaninoff, M-I-E-S-T-C-H-A-N-I-N-O-F-F, born in 1886 in Vitebsk, Russia. [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS] Portrait of the artist's mother. [PAUSES FOR 21 SECONDS] A photograph of the artist in a studio. [PAUSES FOR 18 SECONDS] Felicia Pacanowska, P-A-C-A-N-O-W-S-K-A, born the 15th of January, 1907, in Lodz, Poland. She received her bachelor's degree in 1931, and in 1932 went to Paris. Shown is a painting, date unknown. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] A letter from Paris written in 1956. [PAUSES FOR 28 SECONDS] Page two. [PAUSES FOR 15 SECONDS] Elizabeth Springer, S-P-R-I-N-G-E-R, born in 1904 in Munich. Shown are two paintings, dates unknown. [PAUSES FOR 15 SECONDS] Letter to Dr. Schneid from Springer, date unknown. [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS] Letter, December 1930. [PAUSES FOR 23 SECONDS] Page two. [PAUSES FOR 19 SECONDS] In conclusion, it should be noted that Jewish art was not the only art to be singled out by Hitler as corrupt. Austrian and German expressionists were also designated as degenerate. Artists like Barlach, Dix, Kandinsky, Clay, Kokoschka, and Nolde, to name but a few, also were removed from teaching posts, arrested by the Gestapo, declared degenerate, had works confiscated, and were forbidden to paint. Dr. Schneid's treasure is a priceless legacy not only from an art historical perspective. By the very fact that the treasure exists, the names of many Jewish artists who were lost in the Holocaust and examples of their art, which was destroyed, survive as well.
